content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 21, 2006

Carl G. Borden
California Farm Bureau Federation
Office of the General Counsel
2300 River Plaza Drive
Sacramento, CA 95833-3293

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-205

Dear Mr. Borden:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the El Dorado
County Farm Bureau ("EDCFB") regarding the campaign provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to
past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter will be
construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTIONS

1. Is EDCFB, a membership organization, a recipient committee if it makes an
expenditure of $1,900 in support of a candidate for the county board of supervisors, using
$900 of its own funds and $900 contributed by individual directors of the organization for
that specific purpose?

2. If the answer to the foregoing question is "no," must the organization report
the independent expenditure as having been made only by it, with no disclosure of
information about the directors?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The organization is a recipient committee within the meaning of section 82103
because the contributions to be made by the nine directors will be commingled and be
used to make contributions or expenditures of $1,000 or more in a calendar year.

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-205
Page No. 2

2. Because the answer to the first question is "yes," this question is not
applicable.

FACTS

We were advised in our telephone conversation of October 31, 2006, that
EDCFB, a member organization of the California Farm Bureau Federation, has
authorized you to request advice on its behalf regarding a proposed independent
expenditure in support of a candidate for the county board of supervisors.

EDCFB is a membership organization that has a checking account ("Account")
into which only royalties, rents, and investment income have ever been deposited. No
contributions or member dues have ever been deposited into the Account.

EDCFB will be making an independent expenditure of $1,800 in support of a
candidate for the county board of supervisors. Of this amount, $900 will come from
funds already in the Account. The other $900 will come from contributions by nine of
the organization's directors; each director will give EDCFB a $100 check, which EDCFB
will deposit into the Account. EDCFB will then make the $1,800 independent
expenditure using funds from the Account.

ANALYSIS

The term "contribution" is defined in section 82015 as:

"[A] payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a
payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes."

A payment is made for "political purposes" if it is for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or
election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure.
(Regulation 18215(a)(1).) Accordingly, both the payments made by the individual
directors and payments made by EDCFB of its own funds into the Account constitute
contributions.

Section 82013 defines committee as:

"[A]ny person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly does any of the following:

File No. A-06-205
Page No. 3

"(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year;
"(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a
calendar year; or
"(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to
or at the behest of candidates or committees."

We have previously advised that if a group pools its funds for the purpose of
making contributions or independent expenditures, and the pooled funds of the group
equal $1,000 or more in a calendar year, the group will become a recipient committee.
(Lowell Advice Letter, No. 95-105; Culver Advice Letter, No. I-94-005; Mark Advice
Letter, No. I-90-448.) Thus, the contributions collected by EDCFB in combination with
its own funds that are deposited in the account will cause EDCFB to be a recipient
committee under section 82013(a).2

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:meh
I:\AdviceLtrs\A-06-205

2 The Commission has previously advised that an organization that generates income from sources
other than donations or membership dues (such as sales of goods or services) may count those funds as the
source of contributions and expenditures it subsequently makes and it will not qualify as a recipient
committee. However, in those circumstances, unlike here, the organization received no payments from
other persons. (Fishburn Advice Letter, No. A-06-075).
